Hi Elicit,

I am a solo founder in the same business of epistemic infrastructure. I have built exopriors.com/scry, giving users and their agents arbitrary readonly SQL + compositional vector query power over a large corpus including over 6M embedded abstracts.

This is a very powerful paradigm of research, as people can do _exhaustive_ search they are confident about, which really facilitates updating their world view more than simply finding documents. The compositional vector search also helps people meaningfully compose embeddings in ways they've probably never had the ergonomics of doing so, such as searching for "guilty tones" that aren't biased by the inclusion of the "guilt as a topic".

To make the world a better place, I would be open to sharing my repo with you, and the dozens of hard-earned tricks necessary to give people safe readonly SQL access without headache  on your end. This would allow Elicit to offer a beta Scry-like experience to power users in record time, beating the market to this new paradigm of search/research. All I would ask in return is some kind of trade, maybe $7k, which would give me some real breathing room to do different sorts of foundational epistemic infra work.

Thanks for your important work,

Xyra

additional context:
# ExoPriors, Inc.

Founder: Xyra Sinclair. xyrasinclair@gmail.com

    We are a software company devoted to manifesting foundational subjective judgement infrastructure. We are incredibly long on the "embarrassingly simple" basics of extracting signal from LLMs and facilitating factorized cognitive work at scale. "Putting things in lists better than anyone else". Flushing out meaningful high-dimensional, agent-interopable attribute magnitude scores via pairwise ratio comparisons with care given to intra- and inter-model consistency metrics. Caching quality, relatively expensive units of cognitive work in cheap SQL databases and giving models/users basically arbitrary SQL read access. Setting up pricing systems to incentivize more communally efficient cognitive work. One should be able to upload 500 ML safety papers, have help rating them by high quality iteration on prompts like "readability to a new ML researcher that just grokked transformers", spend the $1 in credits rating them all and caching that useful work for others, and effortlessly do a more refined linear combination and attribute-filtering search of the papers. We're excited to do the blue-collar plumbing to make ecologically empowering use of current LLMs rather than help push the frontiers of training towards uninterpretable monolithic superintelligences.

## MVP: Tweet Explorer

Our initial product focuses on rating and exploring tweets from community-archive.org:

We need a deep reorg of an internal admin dashboard for ExoPriors.  Current state: - Served by Rust API at internal.exopriors.com (root proxied to API). UI is static HTML + vanilla JS at src/api/src/routes/dashboard.html. - Data endpoints: /api/stats (queries + errors + latency), /api/alignment-load, /api/system, /api/pools, /api/margins, /api/page-views/stats, /api/queries/{recent,slow,errors,security}, /api/users, /api/feedback (stats/list), /api/forum-sync, /api/billing/balance, /api/billing/topups, /api/billing/rates, /api/page-views/{recent,top,visitors}. - UI currently has a top bar, a few summary cards, then multiple panels (Scry load, System, Margins, Page Views) and a tab bar that swaps a single large table for: billing, sync, costs, feedback, activity, latency, rejected, queries, accounts, traffic. - There’s a “detail mode” toggle that shows/hides sensitive tabs.  Request: - Deep reorg into a more intuitive layout, dense info (little padding), keep basically all info. Probably in Svelte but can remain HTML if serving constraints. Need a clear information hierarchy.  Ask: - Propose a new layout/IA (information architecture) for this admin panel: what sections, how to group data, recommended structure (grid/columns/panels), and interaction patterns (tabs/accordion/etc) to keep it dense but intuitive. - If you recommend Svelte or staying vanilla, say why given internal domain served by API. - Include a suggested visual layout (e.g., top KPI strip, left ops column, right biz column, query log panel with sub-tabs, etc).

-- Declarative backlog of comparisons to perform
CREATE TABLE comparison_tasks (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v4(),
    attribute_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES attributes(id),
    rater_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES raters(id),
    entity_a_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES entities(id),
    entity_b_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES entities(id),
    job_id UUID REFERENCES rating_jobs(id),
    status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending' CHECK (status IN ('pending','reserved','done','failed','canceled')),
    attempts INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (attempts >= 0),
    last_error TEXT,
    reserved_by TEXT,
    reserved_until TIMESTAMPTZ,
    -- job-scoped result fields (do not mix jobs)
    observed_ln_ratio DOUBLE PRECISION,
    observed_confidence DOUBLE PRECISION,
    vendor_request_id TEXT,
    latency_ms INTEGER,
    completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    -- Canonical orientation for uniqueness
    canon_a_id UUID GENERATED ALWAYS AS (LEAST(entity_a_id, entity_b_id)) STORED,
    canon_b_id UUID GENERATED ALWAYS AS (GREATEST(entity_a_id, entity_b_id)) STORED,
    UNIQUE (job_id, attribute_id, rater_id, canon_a_id, canon_b_id),
    CONSTRAINT different_entities CHECK (entity_a_id <> entity_b_id)
);

count words: As AI agents proliferate, they face a coordination crisis: each must independently rediscover subjective evaluations that others have already made. An agent researching ML papers rates "mathematical difficulty" from scratch; another agent doing the same task a minute later repeats this work entirely. There is no shared infrastructure for subjective measurement—no DNS for qualities like "relevance to AI alignment," "excitingness to a METR researcher," or "fact-checkability." This creates massive redundancy, prevents the compounding of evaluative intelligence, and leaves individual users unable to benefit from collective judgement. Without foundational infrastructure, 99% of future cognitive attention (from agents) will operate in isolated silos rather than building on shared epistemic foundations. Markets won't solve this naturally because it requires standardization on parsimonious primitives—something that demands explicit coordination and careful API design, not competitive fragmentation.

Short answer: not just a statistical anomaly. Raw vs normalized can legitimately differ because you’re changing both the geometry the index was built on and the CPU work in
  the query path. A few concrete reasons:

  - Different geometry = different index graph. Normalizing forces all vectors onto the unit sphere. That changes neighborhood structure, graph connectivity, and search
    complexity for vchordrq. Raw halfvec keeps norm variation, which can make the graph “easier” to traverse (or harder), so latency can move materially.
  - Extra compute if the index isn’t actually used. l2_normalize(subvector(...)) is non‑trivial. If the planner falls back to a seq scan (or the expression doesn’t match the
    index exactly), normalized will be slower just from per‑row math. Your observed “normalized slower than raw” is consistent with that.
  - Caching effects. The first query in a run often pays for disk + cache warmup; the second and third get faster. If raw was run later, it can look faster even with the same
    plan.
  - Operator differences. For cosine on raw vectors, the operator computes norms internally; for pre‑normalized vectors, norms are ~1, but the operator still runs. Depending
    on implementation details, that can still change cost.

  3. Same query vector across both cases.

  Terminalizer

  Similar but with more styling options:

  npm install -g terminalizer

  terminalizer record demo
  # Run your demo
  # Ctrl+D to stop

  # Edit config (change speed, cut frames)
  terminalizer render demo -o output.gif

Why SQL
Most search products hide the query language. We don't.

You describe what you want. Claude writes Structured Query Language—real SQL you can read, edit, save, rerun. Postgres executes it against ~65M documents with proper indexes.

Alignment research queries aren't keyword searches. They're structured questions with lots of constraints and a custom notion of relevance. SQL lets you actually say what you mean.

What you get
Exact filters: date ranges, sources, authors, karma thresholds, tags.
Composability: CTEs let you build pipelines (candidate set → refine → rank).
Hybrid retrieval: lexical + vector similarity in one query.
Vector math: blend concepts, subtract "topic drift," similarity to multiple anchors.
Inspectable: the query is the artifact. Share it, version it, rerun it.
Two-stage retrieval
At 65M docs, you want:

broad candidate set via indexes / ANN
rerank that smaller set with expensive scoring
Not magic
You're not hoping a search box understood you. You describe the target, Claude writes the query, the database returns the list.

 + VECTOR ALGEBRA access over it.
Currently we suggest using your own Claude Code, but we'll be giving you Claude Code in the browser soon."

Subjective Rating Engine

The numbers you've been looking for are here.

LLMs can consistently rate stuff by subjective attributes, and with a sophisticated harness (latent space solver), we can actually put meaningful _cardinal_ numbers on stuff. That means if you rate a dataset by attributes like "delightfully pomp and psychedelic" and "ultra-wattagedness: ultra-wattaged evidence of uncommonly cogent and powerful thinking. Not just pretending to be smart or naively pattern matching to being smart (e.g., mentioning electronics), but actually seeming quite astute.", you can get numbers like the minimum entity being 1, and the maximum being like 1.8 or 12. These numbers are far more meaningful than anything you could extract through Likert scales (e.g. rating 1-5).

We provide a state-of-the-art rating engine that should give you a significant fraction of the signal (our test bench is with a BeatRandom score within 8%, and Spearman correlation above .8) and coherence from N*(N-1)/2 Sonnet comparisons and a quality harness you could whip up, for a third of the cost.

You just post the entities and a subjective attribute_prompt like "unbridled_conscientiousness: exhibiting profound conscientiousness, in action and/or language." to our simple API, and receive back the cardinal numbers with their uncertanties, with a couple global coherence stats to help you understand how well our LLMs are grokking the attribute.

Schema:
  search_vector tsvector GENERATED ALWAYS AS (
      setweight(to_tsvector('english', COALESCE(original_author, '')), 'A') ||
      setweight(to_tsvector('english', COALESCE(metadata->>'title', '')), 'A') ||
      setweight(to_tsvector('english', COALESCE(LEFT(payload, 500000), '')), 'B')
  ) STORED
  - Weight A (high): author + title
  - Weight B: payload content
  - GIN index (2.6GB)

  Supported query functions:
  | Function             | Syntax                                    | Use case                      |
  |----------------------|-------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
  | plainto_tsquery      | 'term1 term2' → ANDs all terms            | Simple keyword search         |
  | websearch_to_tsquery | 'term1 OR term2 -excluded "exact phrase"' | Google-like syntax            |
  | to_tsquery           | `'term1 & term2                           | term3'`                       |
  | phraseto_tsquery     | 'exact phrase order'                      | Words must appear in sequence |

  Ranking:
  - ts_rank(search_vector, query) — basic TF-based relevance
  - ts_rank_cd(search_vector, query) — cover density (rewards proximity)

  Caveat: The Claude prompt says "BM25-style ranking" but that's not quite accurate. PostgreSQL's ts_rank is simpler TF-based, not true BM25 (which would need
  ParadeDB's pg_bm25 extension). Still useful, just not as sophisticated.

  What the Claude prompt currently documents:
  - Section 4 shows plainto_tsquery examples
  - Shows hybrid lexical→semantic approach
  - The landing page uses websearch_to_tsquery in the FTX example

def rank_importance_weights(n: int, alpha: float = 1.3, normalize: bool = True):
    raw = [1.0 / (k ** alpha) for k in range(1, n + 1)]
    if not normalize:
        return raw
    Z = sum(raw)
    return [x / Z for x in raw]

Short answer: don’t assume stored embeddings are L2‑normalized, and after any vector math they definitely aren’t.

  What the repo actually does

  - We do not normalize on insert in the canonical pipelines. continual_scraping/src/bin/voyage_embed.rs stores the Voyage output as‑is (cast to halfvec) and src/api/src/
    routes/embeddings.rs stores /v1/alignment/embed results as‑is. There’s no unit_vector/l2_normalize step there.
  - The only explicit L2‑normalize mention is an optional experimental prefix index (truncate then normalize) in docs/ingestion.md / docs/runbooks/embedding_backfill.md. That
    does not change stored vectors.

  Implications

  - If the model’s output happens to be unit‑length, we preserve that; if not, we store non‑unit vectors. Either way, AVG / + / − / scaling will move you off the unit sphere,
    so you must renormalize composed queries.
  - Our canonical ANN indexes for Voyage use cosine ops (halfvec_cosine_ops) in src/db/schema.sql, so <=> is scale‑invariant; normalization mostly matters for vector algebra
    and for dot/L2 usage, not for cosine ranking itself.

  Quick check: are stored embeddings unit‑length?
  (Uses the built‑in vec_dot helper from src/db/schema.sql.)

  WITH samp AS (
    SELECT embedding
    FROM alignment.embeddings
    WHERE chunk_index = 0 AND embedding IS NOT NULL
    LIMIT 500
  )
  SELECT
    AVG(sqrt(vec_dot(embedding, embedding))) AS avg_norm,
    MIN(sqrt(vec_dot(embedding, embedding))) AS min_norm,
    MAX(sqrt(vec_dot(embedding, embedding))) AS max_norm
  FROM samp;

  How to keep composed vectors well‑behaved

  - Always normalize the composed query vector, not the column (to keep indexes usable):

  WITH q AS (
    SELECT unit_vector(
             scale_vector(@oversight, 0.6) +
             scale_vector(@critique, 0.4)
           ) AS v
  )
  SELECT e.id, e.title, emb.embedding <=> (SELECT v FROM q) AS dist
  FROM alignment.embeddings emb
  JOIN alignment.entities e ON e.id = emb.entity_id
  WHERE emb.chunk_index = 0
  ORDER BY dist
  LIMIT 20;

  If you want me to check a specific query shape you’re using (AVG / debias / centroid), paste it and I’ll tell you where normalization should go and whether the planner will
  still hit the cosine index.
